column2: The total amount the United States spends on healthcare in a year, tracked by CMS and reported as both a dollar total and a share of GDP.
column4: The national health expenditure sits near 17-18% of U.S. GDP, making healthcare one of the largest sectors of the economy.

column2: The total market value of goods and services produced in a country in a year; used as the denominator when expressing healthcare's share of the economy.
column4: Healthcare as a share of gross domestic product has grown for decades, which is why small percentage changes translate into large dollar shifts.

column2: A federal health-insurance program primarily for people age 65 and older and certain people with disabilities, administered by CMS.
column4: Medicare is the largest single purchaser of hospital care in the country, so its payment rules shape how providers operate.

column2: A joint federal-state health-coverage program for people with low income and certain other eligible groups; each state runs its own program within federal rules.
column4: Medicaid is administered jointly by CMS and state agencies, which is why eligibility and covered services vary from state to state.

column2: Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services — the federal agency inside HHS that runs Medicare and co-administers Medicaid.
column4: When CMS changes a payment rule, hospitals across the country adjust workflow and staffing in response.

column2: Food and Drug Administration — the federal agency that regulates the safety and effectiveness of drugs, medical devices, and many foods.
column4: A new medication cannot be sold in the United States until the FDA reviews its safety and effectiveness data.

column2: A payment and delivery approach in which providers are rewarded for patient outcomes and cost-efficiency rather than for the volume of services delivered.
column4: Under value-based care, a health system has a financial reason to prevent readmissions rather than simply bill for more visits.

column2: A single payment that covers all the services tied to one episode of care (for example, a joint replacement and its follow-up), shared across the providers who deliver them.
column4: A bundled payment pushes the surgeon, hospital, and rehab team to coordinate because they share one total payment for the episode.

column2: A government process by which a state grants permission to a person or facility to practice or operate, setting minimum training and conduct standards.
column4: State licensure is why a nurse who moves across state lines often has to take additional steps before practicing.

column2: The organized effort of government and partners to protect and improve the health of whole populations through prevention, surveillance, and environmental control.
column4: Local public health departments run immunization clinics, inspect restaurants, and track disease outbreaks in their county.

column2: A clinic, hospital, or program that delivers care to patients regardless of ability to pay, often supported by federal, state, or local funding.
column4: Community health centers funded through HRSA act as safety-net providers in many underserved neighborhoods.

column2: A factor that pushes healthcare spending up over time — for example, new technology, prescription drug prices, administrative complexity, chronic disease, or an aging population.
column4: Aging and chronic disease are long-run cost drivers that shape state and federal budget projections.
